title: Chocolate stout cake
subtitle: Dense dark chocolate cake with stout and cream cheese frosting
excerpt: A dense, fudgy single-layer chocolate cake made with dark stout and finished with a thick cream cheese frosting. The stout deepens the chocolate flavour without tasting of beer. Serves ten.
sourceNotes: Dark stout used as a liquid in chocolate cake is a modern British baking development. The combination of stout and chocolate exploits the bitter, roasted malt notes shared by both; stout also contributes carbonation that lightens the crumb. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: chocolate cake made with cocoa powder, butter, and eggs is documented in the cake chapter as a British domestic preparation. The stout addition and cream cheese frosting are modern refinements synthesised from widely reproduced public-domain chocolate cake methods and standard cream cheese icing.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted, cooled cake wrapped in cling film for up to 2 months. Frost after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day once the crumb has settled. The frosting can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the cake should feel set at the edges with a very slight wobble at the centre when the tin is moved — it firms as it cools

content.text: The stout in this cake does two things: its bitterness amplifies the chocolate and its carbonation lightens a batter that would otherwise be very dense. The finished cake should not taste of beer. Use a full-bodied dark stout rather than a lighter one; the roasted malt note is what matters.

Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease a 23 cm round tin and line the base. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over low heat. Add the {{stout}} and whisk in the {{cocoa-powder}} until smooth. Remove from the heat and cool for 10 minutes. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Whisk the {{eggs}} and {{soured-cream}} into the cooled stout mixture, then pour into the dry ingredients and whisk until a smooth, liquid batter forms. Pour into the prepared tin.

Bake for 47–52 minutes until the edges are set and a skewer through the centre comes out with just a few moist crumbs. The cake has a slight wobble at the centre when the tin is moved; this is correct. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before turning out onto a rack to cool completely.

Beat the {{cream-cheese}} until smooth. Sift in the {{icing-sugar}} and beat again. Add the {{double-cream}} and beat until the frosting is thick and spreadable. Do not overbeat once the cream is added or it will turn grainy. Spread thickly over the top of the completely cooled cake. Serve at room temperature.
